content: "Well, pretty good, I guess.
I only played one joke."
"What was It?"
Bob related the circumstances of the step ladder, the cook and the hired man.
"Yes," replied Bob, trying not to chuckle at the recollection.
"Hum," remarked the captain again, and he seemed to be having some difficulty with his breathing.
The thought of the sight of the pair tangled up in the step ladder was too much for him, though he did not want to encourage Bob in his reckless ways by showing enough interest to laugh.
"By names, yes, sir.
But I'm afraid I've got lots yet to learn."
Still, he was determined to reform him if it was possible.
"When do you expect to get into the Southern Pacific?" asked Bob, as he thought of the secret map Captain Obed had given him.
Why?"
"Here's something a friend of mine gave me," said Bob, pulling out the wrinkled piece of parchment.
"He says there is treasure buried on an island in the Southern Pacific."
"Treasure?
Captain Spark looked critically at the rather faint tracing of lines on the yellow sheet.
"A joke?"
"Yes.
That's a map, sure enough, but no sailor could ever find the island by those directions."
"Why not?"
"I said he never could.
Why, look, Bob.
Every circle is divided into three hundred and sixty degrees, and as the earth is round.
Each degree therefore means a distance at the equator of about seventy miles.

So unless whoever drew this map is positive that the island is exactly at the intersection of the degrees of latitude and longitude which you have given me, it might be seventy miles one way or the other off from the location given here.
And seventy miles is a good distance on the water.
Besides, the map only states that the location is 'about' right.
I guess we'll never find that treasure, Bob.
I don't take any stock in those Captain Kidd yarns.
There's too many of 'em being spun by retired sailors.
The aged man's mind was not to be depended on.

During the remainder of the journey by rail Captain Spark gave Bob some good advice as to how to conduct himself while aboard the ship. He imparted some useful information concerning navigation, and promised to show Bob more about it after they had sailed.
"I'm anxious to get out on deep water," said the mariner.
"I don't like this city life.
There are too many risks in it."
Not all the freight was aboard yet.
I think we shall be loaded by to morrow."
This lying at dock doesn't suit me.
By the way, let me introduce a friend of mine.
This is Bob Henderson.
His mother is a relative of mine, and Bob is taking a voyage for his health.
Bob, this is my first mate, mr Carr."
"Things are not always what they look like," replied the captain with a smile.
"Bob found matters rather too lively for him ashore, and his folks think it will quiet him down to go with me."
"I'll leave you here a while," went on the commander to the boy. "You can look about a bit while I go below and work on my manifest. mr Carr will tell you anything you want to know."
But Bob was so interested in watching the sailors at work stowing away the cargo, while others were cleaning various parts of the ship, that he did not ask many questions.
All the rest of that day the loading went on.
Captain Spark was below in his cabin, making out the final papers and waiting for his clearance documents from the harbor master.
The man carried a valise in each hand, while behind him walked a 'longshoreman with a trunk on his shoulder.
"Don't drop it for the world."
"Don't do that!" exclaimed the nervous man.
"You might drop it, and something would break."
"All right, sir.
"There you go again!" cried the man in rather whining tones.
"Don't do it, I say!
Keep your hands on the trunk!"
Seeing that this last order was obeyed, the nervous man advanced up the gangplank.
I don't want any mistake made.
I don't see the name on it anywhere."
"It is on the bows and under the stern."
Positive.
A distant relative of my mother is the captain."
"Are you sure?
I don't want to be on the wrong ship."
"Yes, sir, I am very sure, I came on board with him.
Are you going to sail on the ship?" asked Bob politely.
"I expect to, if this is the right vessel.
"No, I am positive.
"Oh, yes.
I shall speak to him."
The nervous man started off.
"All ashore that's going ashore!" called the first mate.
The 'longshoreman waited.
"Oh, dear!
"It is," Bob assured him, smiling at the man's manner.
"Oh, if I have made a mistake and gotten on the wrong ship it will be terrible," murmured the man.
"Why don't you throw off that stern line?" again shouted the captain.
"What shall I do?" exclaimed the nervous man.
"I will, my man.
Here's a dollar.
Never mind the change."
"All ashore that's going ashore!" yelled mr Carr again.
"Are you sure this is the right ship?" appealed the man to Bob once more.
"Positively yes, sir.
"Too late?
How?
What do you mean?"
"I mean that we're under way now."
The tug was straining on the big hawser.
FINDING mr
TARBILL
Bob hurried forward as fast as he could through the water, no longer swimming, but wading.
"I'm on a tropical island," thought the castaway.
Still I ought to be thankful I'm not drowned or eaten by a shark."
Bob was tired after his long swim and stretched out under the trees on the grass to rest.
It was already beginning to get much warmer, though the sun was only just peeping up, seemingly from beneath the ocean.
"Wonder if I'm going to find anything to eat here," the boy thought.
"Doesn't look as if any one lived here.
I wonder if any ships ever pass this place?"
There were so many questions that needed answering he did not know where to stop asking them of himself.
Not that he was afraid of taking cold, but he knew he would be more comfortable in dry garments.
"Now if I could find something to eat I'd be right in it-at least for a while," thought the castaway as he walked around on the warm grass.
Bob walked some distance from where he had landed, keeping a sharp lookout for a spring of water.
All the while he was getting more and more thirsty, and he began to think he would have to dig a little well near shore with clam shells, as he had read of shipwrecked sailors doing.
But, fortunately, he was not forced to this.
"That sounds good," he remarked.

Stepping cautiously, because of his bare feet, he went on a little farther and presently saw a small waterfall, caused by a stream tumbling over a little ledge of rocks and splashing into a pool below.
And a moment later he was drinking his fill.
"Guess I'll try it."
Bob was fond of hunting and fishing and knew considerable about wood lore.
Searching under the stones he soon found some worms, and, tossing one into the middle of the pool, he saw a hungry fish rise to it.
I generally do.
The fish at once rose to the bait, and soon Bob had several beauties on the grass beside him.
He only wished he had more of them.
Then he cleaned the fish and set them to broil by the simple process of hanging them in front of the fire on a pointed stick, one end of which was thrust into the ground.
"I'll not starve here as long as I have fruit and fish."
It seemed to come from behind a pile of rocks off to the left.
"I wonder what that was?" thought Bob.
"An animal or a human being?
I wonder if there are any South Sea natives on this island?"
As he approached, the groans became louder.
For an instant the boy could scarcely believe his eyesight.
Then, with a cry, he rushed forward.
"He, too, must have fallen overboard and been washed ashore.
But he seems to be hurt."
The man's eyes were closed and he was scarcely breathing.
"He's dying!" thought Bob, his heart beating hard.
Then, thinking perhaps the man might be partly drowned, the young castaway began to put into operation as much of the directions as he remembered for restoring partially drowned persons to life.
He had not worked long before he saw mr Tarbill's eyes open.
Then the nervous passenger began to breathe better.
"Where-where am I?" he asked faintly.
"On an island with me.
But where is the captain-and the others?"
"Boat foundered.
Wave washed over it-soon after you fell overboard.
No chance to get life preservers.
"I don't know!
Oh, it is terrible!
I swam as long as I could, then I seemed to be sinking."
He helped mr Tarbill to his feet.
"Those fish smell fine," he said.
He put them once more on the pointed sticks near the fire, and when they were sizzling he laid them on the green leaves.
Then, with sticks for knives and forks, the two castaways made a fairly good meal.
"This has been a terrible experience for me."
"I guess it has," admitted Bob.
That is the last I remember.
We'll search for them.
I guess we're in for a long stay."
"Oh, dear!" exclaimed the nervous man.
"I wish I had stayed at home!"
"I guess that will be the best thing to do.
Bob felt not a little proud of the responsibility thus thrust upon him.
He resolved to act wisely and cautiously, for there was no telling how long they would have to live on the island.
With the boy in the lead the two started off.
He had come to a halt near a pile of rocks and was looking over the tops.
"What's the matter?" asked the boy.
Perhaps they are cannibals!
HE DISCUSSES MAXIMS AND proverbs
"Here's a man after my own heart.
I've always maintained that myself from the moment I had my first copy book lesson in which I had to scrawl the line, 'It's a long lane that has no turning,' twenty four times.
And then that other absurd statement, 'A stitch in the side is worth two in the hand'--or something like it-I forget just how it goes-what Tommy rot that is."
"Well, I don't know about that, mr Idiot," said mr Whitechoker, tapping his fingers together reflectively.
"I should like to agree with you, but I can't," said the Idiot.
"I don't believe anything that is noble in the way of character was ever fostered by such a statement as that it's a long lane that has no turning.
In the first place, it isn't necessarily true.
I know a lane on my grandfather's farm that led from the hen coop to the barn.
There wasn't a turn nor a twist in it, and I know by actual measurement that it wasn't sixty feet long.
The leg that's never pulled doesn't go short in a stringent financial market, and a courtship without a kiss, even if it lasted only five minutes, would be too long for any self respecting lover."
"Perhaps, after all, the idea is ill expressed in the original."
"Perfectly correct," said the Idiot.
"But even then, what?
A boy who didn't know that without being told ought to be spanked and put to bed.
"Oh, well," interposed the Bibliomaniac, "what's the use of being captious?
"There are plenty of others, and the way they refute one another is to me a constant source of delight," said the Idiot.
"There's 'Procrastination is the thief of time,' for instance.
If it's morality you're after I am dead against the teachings of that proverb.
The second thought is the open door to duplicity when it comes to a question of morals.
You ask a small boy, who has been in swimming when he ought to have been at Sunday school, why his shirt is wet.
Sometimes fifth thoughts are best.
"Well, you needn't get mad about it," said the Lawyer.
"I never said it-so you can't blame me."
"Still, there are some proverbs," said mr Whitechoker, blandly, "that we may not so summarily dismiss.
Take, for instance, 'You never miss the water till the well runs dry.'"
"One of the worst of the lot, mr Whitechoker," said the Idiot.
"I've missed the water lots of times when the well was full as ever.
You miss the water when the pipes freeze up, don't you?
Fact is, all these water proverbs are to be regarded with suspicion."
"I don't recall any other," said mr Whitechoker.
And then, 'You may drive a horse to water, but you cannot make him drink,' is another choice specimen of the Waterbury School of Philosophy.
Why not adapt the wisdom of the ancients to modern conditions?

Or if you are interested in having your boy brought up to the strenuous life, why don't you have him make sixty copies of the aphorism, 'A punch in the solar is worth six on the nose?' You tell your children never to whistle until they are out of the woods.
That's where birds whistle.
That's where the wind whistles.

If nature whistles anywhere, it is in the woods. Woods were made for whistling, and any man who ever sat over a big log fire in camp or in library who has not noticed that the logs themselves whistle constantly-well, he is a pachyderm."
"Exactly," said the Idiot.
"But, after all, you've got to have something to begin on.
"Certainly," said the Idiot.
"Here is a list of them."
"You never find the water till the stock falls off twenty points."
"A stitch in time saves nothing at all at present tailors' rates."
"You look after the pennies.
Somebody else will deposit the pounds."
"Second thoughts are always second."
"Procrastination is the theme of gossips."
"Never put off to day what you can put on day after to morrow."
